'''Jim Dymock''' (born 4 April 1972) is a professional [[rugby league]] coach who is the assistant coach of the [[Manly Sea Eagles]] in the [[National Rugby League]] (NRL) and a former professional rugby league footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s.

A [[Tonga national rugby league team|Tonga]] and [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] international, and [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] [[State of Origin series|State of Origin]] representative {{rlp|fe}} or {{rlp|lk}}, he played club football for Sydney's [[Western Suburbs Magpies]], [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] and the [[Parramatta Eels]], finishing his career in the [[Super League]] for the [[London Broncos#1994–2005: Broncos and Super League|London Broncos]].

He then embarked on a coaching career, becoming head coach of the Tongan national team. He spent the latter part of the [[2011 NRL season]] as head coach of the Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs, and has been an assistant coach at the [[Sydney Roosters]], Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs and the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]] in the NRL.<ref>[https://www.titans.com.au/news/2019/09/20/jim-dymock-joins-titans-as-senior-assistant-coach/ Jim Dymock Joins Titans As Senior Assistant Coach]</ref>

==Background==
Dymock was born in [[Sydney]], New South Wales, Australia on 4 April 1972. He is of [[Tonga]]n descent.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.scmp.com/article/101973/dymock-bust |title=Dymock bust-up  |work=South China Morning Post|date=4 January 1995 |access-date=28 May 2023}}</ref>

He began playing rugby league as an [[Eastern Suburbs (Sydney)|Eastern Suburbs]] junior with Paddington Colts and then Woolloomooloo Warriors. He then switched to the [[South Sydney District Junior Rugby Football League|South Sydney juniors competition]]. He played for [[Zetland Magpies]] alongside players such as [[Jim Serdaris]] and [[Terry Hill]] who went on to make First Grade also.

==Playing career==
===Club career===
====Early career====
He represented South Sydney in their S.G. Ball and Jersey Flegg teams.

====Western Suburbs Magpies====
Dymock began his first-grade club career in the [[1991 NSWRL season]] at the [[Western Suburbs Magpies]], opposing [[Wally Lewis]] and scoring two tries in his debut. He played 31 times for the club, but was "glad to go" after troubles with his manager while at [[Western Suburbs Magpies|Wests]].<ref>{{cite journal| first = Daniel| last = Ramus| title = Legend Q&A| journal = [[Rugby League Week]]| issue = 1 July 2009|pages = 30–31| publisher = PBLMedia| location = Sydney, NSW}}</ref>

====Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs====
In 1993, Dymock joined the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]].

During the 1995 season, Dymock, along with [[Dean Pay]], [[Jason Smith (rugby league)|Jason Smith]] and [[Jarrod McCracken]] reneged on their [[Super League War|Australian Super League]] contracts, giving 'unfair inducement' as their reason which was later supported in the courts. Although Dymock chose to sign with the [[Australian Rugby League]] (ARL) competition, he remained with the Canterbury club for the 1995 season and contributed to the club's [[Australian Rugby League season 1995#Grand Final|grand final]] win over [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles|Manly]]. Dymock won the [[Clive Churchill Medal]] for man-of-the-match.

====Parrmatta Eels====
Dymock joined the ARL-aligned Parramatta for the start of the 1996 season.

Dymock was selected to represent [[New South Wales state rugby league team|New South Wales]] as an interchange for all three games of the [[1996 State of Origin series]].

He played for the Eels during the rest of the Super League war and the unification of the Super League and ARL into the current [[National Rugby League]] competition. 
[[File:Jim Dymock.jpg|thumb|right|Dymock as part of the coaching staff at a Bulldogs training session in 2009]]
In 1997, he was selected at five-eighth for games I and II of the [[1997 State of Origin series]], scoring a try in game II, and he was chosen to play at lock in game III of the [[1998 State of Origin series]].

He played 112 games for Parramatta between 1996 and 2000, leaving the Eels and Australia at the end of 2000 season

====London Broncos====
He joined English [[Super League]] club [[London Broncos]] in time for the 2001 season.

He enjoyed 4 seasons at the Broncos. He ended his playing career at the end of the 2004 season after playing 95 games for London.

===International career===
====Tonga====
Dymock represented [[Tonga national rugby league team|Tonga]] at the [[1994 Pacific Cup]] and in 1995.<ref name=book>{{cite book
| last =John Coffey, Bernie Wood
| title = 100 years: Māori rugby league, 1908-2008
| publisher =Huia Publishers
| year = 2008
| pages = 282
| url = https://books.google.com/books?id=nklWo8vw-iIC
| isbn =978-1-86969-331-2
}}</ref>

====Australia====
Dymock also played six times between 1995 and 1996 for [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/league-dymock-running-tongas-rise/FYZQIN544EAC7FHC3PPVHQ45YQ/ |title=League: Dymock running Tonga's rise |work=The New Zealand Herald |date=16 October 2008 |access-date=28 May 2023}}</ref> He was part of the successful Australian squad that won the 1995 [[Rugby League World Cup]] in [[England]].

==Coaching career==
Dymock assisted head coach [[Ricky Stuart]] at the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]].

He later returned to the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]].

Dymock was also the head coach of the [[Tonga national rugby league team|Tongan national rugby league team]] that played in the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]].

On 14 July 2011, Jim Dymock was announced as the Canterbury club's new head coach, after [[Kevin Moore (rugby league)|Kevin Moore]] stood down from the position.<ref name="Tug-of-war over Mason">{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_3935853,00.html|title=Tug-of-war over Mason|publisher=Sky Sports|date=2008-08-05|access-date=2008-08-06}}</ref> However, on 14 November 2011, Dymock was replaced by [[Des Hasler]].<ref>{{cite news| url=http://www.theaustralian.com.au/news/sport/des-hasler-never-contemplated-a-clean-out-at-the-bulldogs/story-e6frg7mf-1226193987021| first=Margie| last=McDonald| title=Des Hasler never contemplated a clean-out at the Bulldogs| date=14 November 2011| work=The Australian}}</ref>
